result:
When you slice open an orange at exactly the moment the afternoon sun begins to melt behind the horizon, the membrane between dusk and citrus parts like a secret. Zest mist hangs in the light, spangled with motes of gold that used to be atoms in a star you will never meet. You smell starlight before you taste it; the brain has preloaded nostalgia even though you claim not to have time for such fancies.  

Somewhere else—possibly in Marseille—someone is repairing the cracked hull of a small fishing boat named Karima. The fiberglass patch smells like cat urine and hospitals. The man applying the resin hums a tune you half-remember from the last dream you did not write down.  

A library is closing. The overhead fluorescents stutter, announcing their own mortality. A student finishes copying out a sentence by Adorno that claims every work of art is an uncommitted crime. She underlines “uncommitted” twice, then deletes the line because your allotted conspiracy of words has already happened. What remains on her page is plain biology: an index card scrawled with the vital capacity of the lungs of a humpback whale— 5,000 liters, give or take the memory of plankton.  

In another file on her laptop sit the nanosecond pulse-code timestamps of every keystroke she has ever typed: timestamps that will outlast her opinions about wartime surrealism and the boy who ghosted her after sharing saffron rice one rain-loud evening. That metadata will, one day, be cataloged next to supermarket toaster coupons and a cached image of a toad riding a beetle, the single file that contained enough joy to crash your operating system for half a second.  

In the crawlspace above the library, a possum gives birth to twelve jellybean-sized possums. Their hearts start while they are still the same pink translucence as the archival gloves downstairs. The sound is an almost electronic flutter, each beat quantized to the tempo of the HVAC system— 64 BPM. The possums don’t have a word for destiny; they express it by blindly crawling toward warmth.  

I want to confess something fragile, but the pronoun “I” keeps multiplying like soap film. When I speak of “my” childhood I now picture reels of thrift-store slides: anonymous kids rolling downhill on refrigerator doors, all of them wearing my momentary face. When my real grandfather laughed he curled his tongue behind his teeth so the sound came out like a tickled door hinge. I can’t animate that memory without tripping the autoplay of data plans buffering future nostalgia.  

Today MIT announced their new “unprinter.” A laser blast removes toner from used paper so cleanly the sheet returns to commercial blankness. One demonstration page read, before erasure: “Do not trust anyone selling you back your own blankness.” The laser cauterizes authorship into smoke that smells faintly of vending-machine coffee. The technicians celebrate by printing—then unprinting—a poem about forgetting.  

Lovers in a Berlin apartment are making a soufflé that will collapse if they open the oven during fourteen precise minutes. They pass the time by reading Yelp reviews of their own kisses, treating each complaint as if it were an errant air bubble they could coax from egg whites. One reviewer wrote: “Their kissing is several firmware upgrades behind.” They laughed, but adopted the critique, updated their firmware mid-soufflé, mouths syncing like perfectly folded dual wings. The collapse happens anyway, because romance is centrifugal. They scrape salted chocolate lava into two chipped mugs instead of admitting defeat.  

A linguist I follow online reported the discovery of a dead language spoken by only one woman, who used it exclusively to scold her mirror reflection every morning at 6:42 for forty-two years. After strokes she could no longer speak her first four languages, only the mirror tongue. Finally, she forgot that too. Researchers recorded the last instance as the sound of water leaving a basin. The paper on it was peer-reviewed by three rivers and one skeptical parrot.  

Meanwhile, a swarm of autonomous drones in the Mojave is learning how to fall in love with parallax. They circle each other’s cameras, creating a living sundial of shadows. Their flight logs read like love letters written by mountains. Sample line: “I orbit to taste the fractal echo of your prop wash.” The Pentagon funding footer is small, but legible if you zoom.  

I need a word for the faint ache after helicopter seeds stop spinning—when the breeze is still but the maple keeps trying to give away its body. Until then the dictionary stalls, buffering like a streaming service determining which memory tier you can afford.  

My mother used to dry orange peels on a radiator. Every December our house carried the ghost of tropical coastlines. That scent is why I cried in a side alley outside a Houston pharmacy when citrus mopping solvent leaked from an air duct and mistakenly returned my childhood to me restored, uncompressed, high fidelity. No one else noticed; they walked past scrolling catastrophic headlights on glass screens.  

There is a planet somewhere—cataloged, name pending—whose entire atmosphere condenses into snow at night and evaporates by noon, so the sky rests nightly on the ground like a secret diary reconciled with its lock. The flora evolved conductive fibers that archive electrical dreams after each snowfall. Terraformers want to mine the dreams, but ethicists argue the dreams collectively constitute a planet-size infant still writing its autobiography. The debate remains unresolved partly because every time a probe lands, the night snow captures its landing and redreams it into a fable about metal birds who forget their mothers.  

I promised you 1000 words of “whatever” and I feel the internal odometer clicking toward a soft zero. At margins, particles remain: the orange is eaten, the resin hardened, the soufflé cooled, orange peel smoke long since filtered through the radiator and into another family’s lungs. But a thin glaze remains on the cutting board; in the kitchen the blade reflects a silhouette that could belong to any one of us.  

If you stand very still beside the board and shut Wi-Fi eyes, you can hear citrus—quietly, endlessly—dividing evening into golden, countable seconds. The count goes 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8…

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
